ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Vietnamese frog hotpot. The general hot pot food product class (FOODON_03315595) is used as the closest defensible ancestor. Frog-specific hotpot is not individually enumerated in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Frog legs — used as the primary protein anchor for this hotpot dish; the composite hotpot preparation is not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated composite code for Vietnamese frog hotpot. A0FJP (frog legs) is used as the primary ingredient anchor. The composite dish preparation is documented here as an approximation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for frog hotpot or Vietnamese hotpot dishes as of 2024.

serving.description: 1 individual bowl (approximately 400g, portioned from a shared hotpot set for 2–3 people)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual serving of frog hotpot is approximately 400g, comprising frog legs, broth, vegetables, mushrooms, and optional rice vermicelli, portioned from a communal hotpot set designed for 2–3 people.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Frog legs, raw (FDC ID 174474) combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table estimates for Vietnamese hotpot broth and vegetable components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No approved source contains a direct composite entry for Vietnamese frog hotpot. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central frog legs (FDC ID 174474) as the primary protein component, weighted with FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable and broth component estimates. The input record values (energy 120 kcal, protein 10g, fat 6g, carbohydrate 6.67g per 100g) are broadly consistent with this composite estimation and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 300mg/100g reflects fish sauce and broth contribution and is plausible for a hotpot preparation. All values represent the ready-to-eat composite dish including broth, frog legs, and vegetables in typical proportions.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283 — analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli (GI ~53) and mixed vegetable broth components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese frog hotpot in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated at approximately 52 (low category) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being rice vermicelli (GI ~53, University of Sydney GI Database) and the moderating effect of high protein (10g/100g from frog legs), dietary fibre (1.33g/100g from vegetables), and the broth-based preparation which slows gastric emptying. Glycemic load per 400g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (52 × 26.68) / 100 ≈ 13.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300mg/100g from fish sauce and broth; a 400g serving delivers approximately 1200mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce in the broth, limit broth consumption to reduce cumulative sodium intake, choose rice vermicelli over instant noodles (which add further sodium), and monitor total daily sodium from all meal sources.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (6.67g/100g), low sugar (1.0g/100g), and high protein (10g/100g) from frog legs support postprandial glucose stability; estimated GI of 52 (low category) further reduces glycaemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume frog hotpot with portion awareness, choosing a smaller rice vermicelli portion or substituting with additional vegetables, avoiding sweet dipping sauces, and limiting pineapple quantity in the broth to minimise added sugar and moderate-GI fruit contribution.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 2.0g/100g and trans fat is 0g/100g, which is favourable; however, sodium at 300mg/100g and cholesterol at 40mg/100g require monitoring for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume frog hotpot in moderate portions, prioritising the lean frog leg protein and vegetables while limiting broth volume to reduce sodium load, avoiding high-sodium condiments, and choosing rice vermicelli over instant noodles to minimise refined carbohydrate and additional sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Frog legs are a lean white protein with low arachidonic acid burden; anti-inflammatory ingredients including lemongrass, galangal, garlic, mushrooms (beta-glucans), and fresh herbs (basil, coriander) provide polyphenols that may support inflammation modulation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor tolerance to nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, chilli) which may trigger flares in sensitive individuals, request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-related tissue swelling, and choose rice vermicelli over refined instant noodles to avoid pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrate load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Frog Hotpot (Vietnamese: Lẩu Ếch) is a traditional Vietnamese communal hotpot dish featuring tender frog legs as the primary protein, simmered in a fragrant and savoury broth typically seasoned with lemongrass, galangal, garlic, chilli, and fish sauce. The dish is served with a variety of fresh vegetables such as water spinach, bean sprouts, and tomatoes, along with mushrooms, pineapple, and fresh herbs including basil and coriander. Diners cook the ingredients tableside in the shared hotpot broth, often accompanied by rice vermicelli or noodles. Frog legs have a mild, delicate flavour and a texture similar to chicken, making them a lean white protein source with approximately 10g protein per 100g of the composite dish. The broth-based preparation results in a relatively low energy density of approximately 120 kcal per 100g. The dish has a moderate sodium content of 300mg/100g primarily from fish sauce and broth, which requires attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 52 (low category) reflects the moderating effect of high protein, dietary fibre from vegetables, and the broth medium on carbohydrate absorption. Frog hotpot is a popular dish in Vietnam and is also served in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is suitable for communal dining and is typically ordered as a set for 2–3 people, with a total portion weight of approximately 1500g. The dish is dairy-free and can be made gluten-free by substituting rice vermicelli for noodles and ensuring fish sauce is gluten-free.

confidence_reason: No approved primary source contains a direct composite entry for Vietnamese frog hotpot. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central frog legs data (FDC ID 174474) combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable and broth component estimates. The input record values are broadly consistent with this estimation and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli as the dominant carbohydrate source. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for Vietnamese frog hotpot composite dish not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA database as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese hotpot dish are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible — frog legs and vegetable-based hotpot preparation do not contain industrially produced trans fats; value is consistent with USDA FoodData Central frog legs entry. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, which likely reflects the restaurant context (GrabFood Singapore listing) rather than the dish's culinary origin; region_of_origin is correctly recorded as Vietnam. The input ethnic_tags included Pho and Banh Mi which are unrelated dishes and have been removed. The rheumatoid_arthritis health suitability entry in the input had an internally contradictory suitable:false flag despite the reason text describing anti-inflammatory benefits; this has been corrected to suitable:true (low concern) in the additional_conditions section with appropriate clinical guidance. Nutrient values represent the ready-to-eat composite dish including broth, frog legs, and vegetables in typical proportions for a Vietnamese frog hotpot preparation.
